content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                                    Date : 6-29-77

SUPERSEDED

Ref: 95-201

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Mike Baker

Subject:

The following question was considered at the opinion request
meeting of June 20, 1977. A lobbyist and a legislator have
dinner together. The lobbyist pays for the dinner on his
credit card and the legislator reimburses the lobbyist at a
later time. Does the lobbyist have any reporting obligations
in connection with this transaction? Is a violation of the
gift prohibition contained in Section 86203 involved in this
transaction?

It was decided that the lobbyist had no disclosure obligation
with respect to this transaction if the legislator repaid the
lobbyist prior to the closing date of the lobbyist's monthly
report. If the transaction occurs less than a week prior to
the closing date for a report, it was decided that no report-
ing obligation would arise if the legislator repaid the lob-
byist within one week.

It was also decided that no violation of the gift prohibition
would occur even if the debt remained unpaid beyond the clos-
ing date of the lobbyist's statement provided that the legis-
lator actually intended to repay the lobbyist and there was
no suggestion that a gift was involved.

MJ:JO

cc: Al Burnham

sections.question: A lobbyist and a legislator have dinner together. The lobbyist pays for the dinner on his credit card and the legislator reimburses the lobbyist at a later time. Does the lobbyist have any reporting obligations in connection with this transaction? Is a violation of the gift prohibition contained in Section 86203 involved in this transaction?
sections.conclusion: It was decided that the lobbyist had no disclosure obligation with respect to this transaction if the legislator repaid the lobbyist prior to the closing date of the lobbyist's monthly report. If the transaction occurs less than a week prior to the closing date for a report, it was decided that no reporting obligation would arise if the legislator repaid the lobbyist within one week. It was also decided that no violation of the gift prohibition would occur even if the debt remained unpaid beyond the closing date of the lobbyist's statement provided that the legislator actually intended to repay the lobbyist and there was no suggestion that a gift was involved.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a lobbyist who pays for a legislator's dinner and is later reimbursed has reporting obligations and whether this transaction violates gift prohibition statutes.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The lobbyist has no reporting obligation if the legislator reimburses before the report closing date, or within one week if the dinner occurred close to the deadline. No gift prohibition violation occurs if the legislator genuinely intends to repay, even if unpaid beyond the reporting deadline.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format from 1977. No explicit section headers but question and conclusion clearly demarcated. Document appears complete with initials and cc notation at end.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A lobbyist and a legislator have dinner together. The lobbyist pays for the dinner on his credit card and the legislator reimburses the lobbyist at a later time. Does the lobbyist have any reporting obligations in connection with this transaction? Is a violation of the gift prohibition contained in Section 86203 involved in this transaction?

CONCLUSION: It was decided that the lobbyist had no disclosure obligation with respect to this transaction if the legislator repaid the lobbyist prior to the closing date of the lobbyist's monthly report. If the transaction occurs less than a week prior to the closing date for a report, it was decided that no reporting obligation would arise if the legislator repaid the lobbyist within one week. It was also decided that no violation of the gift prohibition would occur even if the debt remained unpaid beyond the closing date of the lobbyist's statement provided that the legislator actually intended to repay the lobbyist and there was no suggestion that a gift was involved.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum To : File Date : 6-29-77 SUPERSEDED Ref: 95-201 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Mike Baker Subject: The following question was considered at the opinion request meeting of June 20, 1977. A lobbyist and a legislator have dinner together. The lobbyist pays for the dinner on his credit card and the legislator reimburses the lobbyist at a later time. Does the lobbyist have any reporting obligations in connection with this transaction? Is a violation of the gift prohibition contained in Section 86203 involved in this transaction? It was decided that the lobbyist had no disclosure obligation with respect to this transaction if the legislator repaid the lobbyist prior to the closing date of the lobbyist's monthly report. If the transaction occurs less than a week prior to the closing date for a report, it was decided that no report- ing obligation would arise if the legislator repaid the lob- byist within one week. It was also decided that no violation of the gift prohibition would occur even if the debt remained unpaid beyond the clos- ing date of the lobbyist's statement provided that the legis- lator actually intended to repay the lobbyist and there was no suggestion that a gift was involved. MJ:JO cc: Al Burnham
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether a lobbyist who purchases a legislator's dinner and receives reimbursement has reporting obligations or violates gift prohibitions under Section 86203. The determination turns on whether reimbursement occurs before the reporting deadline and whether the transaction was genuinely a loan rather than a gift.
